channel.title: PyMathon
channel.description: Learn mathematics through clear proofs, JEE problem solving, Python verification, interactive visualisations, and carefully explained examples.

item.title: When a Sumset Is as Small as Possible
item.dc:creator: Ashish K. Srivastava
item.description:
The problem that starts our investigation

Let

be a set of integers satisfying

Define

If contains exactly elements, find

NoteQuestion source

This question appeared in JEE (Main) 2022, 28 June, Morning Shift (Shift 1), Mathematics.
View the original-question listing

At first, the information seems insufficient. We know only the first and last elements of , not the eighteen integers in between. The number is the clue: it is the smallest possible size of . A set whose sumset is this small must have a very special structure.

By the end of the article, we will prove that structure rather than guess it. The final answer will be

Difficulty: Intermediate
Prerequisites: Sets, inequalities, arithmetic progressions, and basic Python
Main idea: Few distinct pairwise sums mean many sums coincide. Those coincidences force equal consecutive gaps.

1. What is a sumset?

If and are sets of numbers, their sumset is

The notation means that both addends are chosen from the same set. Repetition is allowed when choosing the two addends: is included. However, the result is a set, so a value is written only once even if several pairs produce it.

A small example

Take

The addition table is

Therefore,

and . The vertical bars mean “number of elements”.

There are ordered choices of two elements, but only five distinct answers. For example,

Such equalities are called collisions of sums.

Now compare this with :

Thus . The irregular gap from to creates an additional distinct sum. This hints at the principle we will prove:

Equally spaced sets have the smallest possible sumsets.

2. Ordering gives a guaranteed chain of sums

Let a set of distinct real numbers be written in increasing order:

Every number below belongs to :

Why is the middle inequality strict? Since ,

The chain contains

different sums. Consequently:

ImportantSumset lower bound

For every finite set of distinct real numbers,

This proof needs only ordering. In particular, it works for sets of integers, rational numbers, or real numbers.

What the theorem does—and does not—say

The theorem does not claim that always has exactly elements. It says that is the minimum possible.

For :

has elements, while

has six.

3. Arithmetic progressions attain the lower bound

Suppose is an arithmetic progression:

Choose the terms with indices and , where . Their sum is

Only the value of matters. Its possible values are

which gives exactly distinct sums. In fact,

So is itself an arithmetic progression, with the same common difference.

For example,

gives

Here and .

4. The equality case: minimum size forces an AP

We have shown one direction:

The surprising part is the converse.

ImportantMinimal-sumset theorem

For a finite set of real numbers, with ,

We now prove the difficult direction in small steps.

Step 1: The master chain already fills the sumset

Recall the sums

If , there is no room for any additional value. Therefore every sum must equal one of the values in this master chain.

Step 2: Insert sums made with

Consider

Its endpoints are already in the master chain:

and is the value immediately after .

Between these endpoints, the master chain contains exactly

which are values. The second chain also contains exactly interior values:

There are no other sumset values available. Both lists are strictly increasing, so their terms must match in order:

Rearranging gives

The same equality is automatically true for . Thus

All consecutive gaps are equal, which is precisely the definition of an arithmetic progression. This completes the proof.

The central intuition

A randomly spaced set creates many distinct sums. To reach the absolute minimum , every sum outside the guaranteed master chain must collide with a sum already in it. The first row of necessary collisions forces

and therefore forces every consecutive gap to be the same.

5. Solving the original problem

The set

contains

elements. Hence the general lower bound is

We are told that , so equality occurs. By the minimal-sumset theorem, must be an arithmetic progression.

Let its common difference be . There are equal gaps from the first term to the twentieth term, so

Therefore,

Thus

and the requested terms are

Their sum is

There is also a quick symmetry check. The sum of all terms is

Removing the two endpoints gives

6. Explore sumsets with Python

The mathematics proves the result for every valid set. Python is useful for experimenting with examples, seeing collisions, and checking conjectures.

A basic sumset tool

Hide code 
def sumset(A, B=None):
    """Return the sorted sumset A+B; use B=A when B is omitted."""
    A = set(A)
    B = A if B is None else set(B)
    return sorted(a + b for a in A for b in B)


A = {1, 3, 5}
B = {1, 3, 8}

print("A + A =", sumset(A))
print("|A + A| =", len(sumset(A)))
print("B + B =", sumset(B))
print("|B + B| =", len(sumset(B)))
A + A = [2, 4, 4, 6, 6, 6, 8, 8, 10]
|A + A| = 9
B + B = [2, 4, 4, 6, 9, 9, 11, 11, 16]
|B + B| = 9

Using a Python set is essential: if the same sum arises from several pairs, it must still be counted only once.

Find which pairs collide

Hide code 
from collections import defaultdict


def sum_representations(A):
    """Map each sum to unordered pairs that produce it."""
    values = sorted(set(A))
    representations = defaultdict(list)

    for i, x in enumerate(values):
        for y in values[i:]:
            representations[x + y].append((x, y))

    return dict(sorted(representations.items()))


for total, pairs in sum_representations({1, 3, 5}).items():
    print(f"{total:2} can be made by {pairs}")
 2 can be made by [(1, 1)]
 4 can be made by [(1, 3)]
 6 can be made by [(1, 5), (3, 3)]
 8 can be made by [(3, 5)]
10 can be made by [(5, 5)]

This prints, among other lines,

 6 can be made by [(1, 5), (3, 3)]

The ordered pair is not printed separately because addition is commutative.

Test the theorem on examples

Hide code 
def is_arithmetic_progression(A):
    values = sorted(set(A))
    if len(values) < 3:
        return True

    d = values[1] - values[0]
    return all(
        values[i + 1] - values[i] == d
        for i in range(len(values) - 1)
    )


def report(A):
    values = sorted(set(A))
    n = len(values)
    size = len(sumset(values))
    print("A =", values)
    print(f"|A| = {n}, |A+A| = {size}, lower bound = {2*n - 1}")
    print("Arithmetic progression?", is_arithmetic_progression(values))


report({1, 5, 9, 13})
print()
report({1, 5, 9, 14})
A = [1, 5, 9, 13]
|A| = 4, |A+A| = 16, lower bound = 7
Arithmetic progression? True

A = [1, 5, 9, 14]
|A| = 4, |A+A| = 16, lower bound = 7
Arithmetic progression? False

Changing the last element from to breaks the equal spacing and makes the sumset larger.

Search all small sets

The next experiment lists every four-element subset of whose sumset has the minimum size .

Hide code 
from itertools import combinations

n = 4
minimum = 2 * n - 1
minimal_sets = []

for candidate in combinations(range(9), n):
    if len(sumset(candidate)) == minimum:
        minimal_sets.append(candidate)

print("Sets with a minimum-size sumset:")
for candidate in minimal_sets:
    print(candidate)

print(
    "Every result is an AP:",
    all(is_arithmetic_progression(A) for A in minimal_sets),
)
Sets with a minimum-size sumset:
Every result is an AP: True

This is experimental evidence, not a substitute for the proof. A finite search checks only the cases inside its chosen range; the theorem covers all finite sets of real numbers.

Verify the contest problem directly

Hide code 
A = list(range(1, 78, 4))
AA = sumset(A)

print("A =", A)
print("|A| =", len(A))
print("A+A =", AA)
print("|A+A| =", len(AA))
print("Sum of the 18 interior terms =", sum(A[1:-1]))
A = [1, 5, 9, 13, 17, 21, 25, 29, 33, 37, 41, 45, 49, 53, 57, 61, 65, 69, 73, 77]
|A| = 20
A+A = [2, 6, 6, 10, 10, 10, 14, 14, 14, 14, 18, 18, 18, 18, 18, 22, 22, 22, 22, 22, 22, 26, 26, 26, 26, 26, 26, 26, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 34, 34, 34, 34, 34, 34, 34, 34, 34, 38, 38, 38, 38, 38, 38, 38, 38, 38, 38, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 46, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 50, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 54, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 58, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 62, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 66, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 70, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 74, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 78, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 82, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 86, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 90, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 94, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 98, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 102, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 106, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 110, 114, 114, 114, 114, 114, 114, 114, 114, 114, 114, 114, 118, 118, 118, 118, 118, 118, 118, 118, 118, 118, 122, 122, 122, 122, 122, 122, 122, 122, 122, 126, 126, 126, 126, 126, 126, 126, 126, 130, 130, 130, 130, 130, 130, 130, 134, 134, 134, 134, 134, 134, 138, 138, 138, 138, 138, 142, 142, 142, 142, 146, 146, 146, 150, 150, 154]
|A+A| = 400
Sum of the 18 interior terms = 702

The program produces elements in , elements in , and an interior sum of .

7. A broader viewpoint

This problem is a first glimpse of additive combinatorics, the study of how the arithmetic structure of a set is reflected in sets such as and .

The quantity

is called the doubling constant of . A small doubling constant suggests that is highly structured. In our extreme one-dimensional case,

forces the strongest possible structure: an arithmetic progression.

You do not need additive combinatorics to solve the original question. Still, it is useful to see the larger mathematical theme:

The number of distinct sums reveals how regularly the original set is arranged.

8. Common mistakes

Counting pairs instead of distinct sums

is a set of values, not a list of pairs. Although there are ordered pairs , different pairs may give the same sum.

Forgetting that an element may be used twice

Because and are independently chosen from , sums such as are allowed.

Assuming the AP structure without proving equality

The lower bound alone does not make an AP. We need the given equality and the equality-case theorem.

Miscounting the number of terms

The set in the problem has terms:

Therefore it has consecutive gaps, not .

9. Try it yourself

  1. Find when . Verify that its size is .
  2. If has distinct real elements, what is the smallest possible value of ?
  3. Let be a set of integers with . Find .
  4. Compare the sumset sizes of and . Which new sums appear after the last term is changed?
TipHints
  1. Write the sums in increasing order; the answer is also an AP.
  2. Use .
  3. There are six elements, and .
  4. Use the Python sumset function or construct two addition tables.
NoteAnswers
  1. , so .
  2. .
  3. Equality forces an AP. Its common difference is , so the interior terms are and their sum is .
  4. The sizes are and , respectively. The first sumset is ; the second is .

Conclusion

For a set of distinct ordered numbers, the chain formed from the smallest and largest elements guarantees at least distinct pairwise sums. Arithmetic progressions attain this bound, and attaining the bound forces all consecutive gaps to be equal.

In the original problem, and . Hence must be

so

item.title: Longest Collatz Sequence
item.dc:creator: Ashish K. Srivastava
item.description:
The Collatz rule is easy to state, but its long-term behaviour is still not understood. We begin with the rule itself, then use it to solve the finite search problem posed in Project Euler Problem 14.

The Collatz Process

For a positive integer, apply one of two operations: halve an even number, and replace an odd number by three times the number plus one. Repeating this instruction produces a Collatz sequence, also called an orbit.

Let . The Collatz map is

Starting at , the successive values are . Here means that the map has been applied times.

The Collatz Conjecture

The conjecture states that every positive starting value eventually reaches :

Once the value is reached, iteration continues around the familiar cycle

For chain lengths, we stop at the first occurrence of .

Why this is still a conjecture

Computers have checked the rule for extraordinarily many starting values. That is convincing evidence, but it is not a proof. A finite computation can only check finitely many integers; the statement concerns every positive integer.

A proof would have to rule out both an orbit that grows without bound and a non-trivial cycle other than . Neither has been ruled out for all positive integers. The original Collatz conjecture therefore remains a conjecture, not a theorem.

A first example:

Applying the rule carefully gives

This sequence contains

and takes

The distinction matters throughout this article:

Generating Collatz Sequences with Python

Before writing a search program, we should be able to generate one orbit correctly. The function below checks that its input is a positive integer and returns the sequence up to the first .

Hide code 
def collatz_sequence(n):
    """Return the Collatz sequence from positive integer n to its first 1."""
    if not isinstance(n, int) or isinstance(n, bool) or n < 1:
        raise ValueError("n must be a positive integer")

    sequence = [n]
    while n != 1:
        if n % 2 == 0:
            n //= 2
        else:
            n = 3 * n + 1
        sequence.append(n)
    return sequence


sequence_13 = collatz_sequence(13)
print(sequence_13)
print(f"Terms: {len(sequence_13)}")
print(f"Steps: {len(sequence_13) - 1}")
[13, 40, 20, 10, 5, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1]
Terms: 10
Steps: 9

The output agrees with the hand calculation: has ten terms and nine steps.

For a few selected values, it is useful to record the chain length and the largest value reached. This is experimental verification of particular cases, not a proof of the conjecture.

Hide code 
def collatz_summary(n):
    """Return basic information about the Collatz sequence starting at n."""
    sequence = collatz_sequence(n)
    return {
        "start": n,
        "terms": len(sequence),
        "steps": len(sequence) - 1,
        "maximum": max(sequence),
    }


test_values = [6, 10, 13, 19, 27, 97]
summaries = [collatz_summary(n) for n in test_values]
for summary in summaries:
    print(summary)
{'start': 6, 'terms': 9, 'steps': 8, 'maximum': 16}
{'start': 10, 'terms': 7, 'steps': 6, 'maximum': 16}
{'start': 13, 'terms': 10, 'steps': 9, 'maximum': 40}
{'start': 19, 'terms': 21, 'steps': 20, 'maximum': 88}
{'start': 27, 'terms': 112, 'steps': 111, 'maximum': 9232}
{'start': 97, 'terms': 119, 'steps': 118, 'maximum': 9232}
Start Terms Steps Maximum value
6 9 8 16
10 7 6 16
13 10 9 40
19 21 20 88
27 112 111 9232
97 119 118 9232

The chain beginning at is worth noticing. It starts small, rises as high as , and only then returns to . This is a useful reminder that the rule does not produce a steadily decreasing sequence.

Hide code 
sequence_27 = collatz_sequence(27)
print(f"Terms: {len(sequence_27)}")
print(f"Steps: {len(sequence_27) - 1}")
print(f"Maximum value: {max(sequence_27)}")
Terms: 112
Steps: 111
Maximum value: 9232

Trajectory for

A plot makes the rise and fall of this orbit easier to see.

Hide code 
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'svg'
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#0c1521")
ax.set_facecolor("#101d2d")
ax.plot(range(len(sequence_27)), sequence_27, linewidth=1.5, color="#a9ecf4")
ax.set_xlabel("Step number", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_ylabel("Term value", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_title("Collatz trajectory for n = 27", color="#f1f6fb")
ax.tick_params(colors="#d5e1eb")
for spine in ax.spines.values():
    spine.set_color("#5d7590")
ax.grid(True, color="#7890a8", alpha=0.25)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Project Euler Problem 14

The Project Euler question is finite. It asks:

Among all starting integers satisfying

find the starting value that produces the longest Collatz chain before reaching .

Only the starting number must be below one million. Terms later in the chain may be much larger. This is important: the eventual winner rises to more than two billion.

The Mathematical Structure of the Search

Define

Then

and, for ,

Equivalently,

Thus the required value is

The key observation is that chains share tails. Once a chain reaches a previously studied value, the remaining work is already known. For example, if is known, then

and

This recurrence is the mathematical reason that memoization works. Before we write the efficient program, it is useful to see the direct method.

A Direct Python Solution

The direct method constructs a complete chain for every possible starting value. It is correct, but it repeatedly computes common tails such as .

Hide code 
def longest_collatz_naive(limit):
    """Return the start and term count of the longest chain below limit."""
    if not isinstance(limit, int) or isinstance(limit, bool) or limit <= 1:
        raise ValueError("limit must be an integer greater than 1")

    best_start, best_length = 1, 1
    for n in range(1, limit):
        current_length = len(collatz_sequence(n))
        if current_length > best_length:
            best_start, best_length = n, current_length
    return best_start, best_length


print(longest_collatz_naive(1_000))
(871, 179)

For a small limit this is perfectly adequate. At one million starts, however, the repeated tails become expensive. The recurrence for tells us exactly what to store.

Memoization

Start with the known value . When a new path reaches a value already stored in a cache, work backwards through the new part of the path. Each predecessor has a chain one term longer than its successor.

Hide code 
def collatz_length(n, cache):
    """Return the term count for n, extending cache along a new tail."""
    if not isinstance(n, int) or isinstance(n, bool) or n < 1:
        raise ValueError("n must be a positive integer")

    original_n = n
    path = []
    while n not in cache:
        path.append(n)
        if n % 2 == 0:
            n //= 2
        else:
            n = 3 * n + 1

    known_length = cache[n]
    for value in reversed(path):
        known_length += 1
        cache[value] = known_length
    return cache[original_n]


def longest_collatz(limit):
    """Return the start, term count, and cache for chains below limit."""
    if not isinstance(limit, int) or isinstance(limit, bool) or limit <= 1:
        raise ValueError("limit must be an integer greater than 1")

    cache = {1: 1}
    best_start, best_length = 1, 1
    for n in range(1, limit):
        current_length = collatz_length(n, cache)
        if current_length > best_length:
            best_start, best_length = n, current_length
    return best_start, best_length, cache

Now we can translate the recurrence into Python. The following finite computation solves the Project Euler search; it does not claim to prove the Collatz conjecture.

Hide code 
best_start, best_length, length_cache = longest_collatz(1_000_000)
print(f"Starting number: {best_start}")
print(f"Number of terms: {best_length}")
print(f"Number of steps: {best_length - 1}")
Starting number: 837799
Number of terms: 525
Number of steps: 524
NoteFinal result

is the starting value below one million with the longest Collatz chain. The chain contains

and requires

Inspecting the Winning Sequence

The winning chain begins with large oscillations but ends in the familiar tail through .

Hide code 
winning_sequence = collatz_sequence(best_start)
print("First 15 terms:")
print(winning_sequence[:15])
print("\nLast 15 terms:")
print(winning_sequence[-15:])
print(f"\nMaximum value reached: {max(winning_sequence):,}")
First 15 terms:
[837799, 2513398, 1256699, 3770098, 1885049, 5655148, 2827574, 1413787, 4241362, 2120681, 6362044, 3181022, 1590511, 4771534, 2385767]

Last 15 terms:
[70, 35, 106, 53, 160, 80, 40, 20, 10, 5, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1]

Maximum value reached: 2,974,984,576
Hide code 
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'svg'
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#0c1521")
ax.set_facecolor("#101d2d")
ax.plot(range(len(winning_sequence)), winning_sequence, linewidth=1.35, color="#ffe09a")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Step number", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_ylabel("Term value (logarithmic scale)", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_title("Collatz trajectory for n = 837,799", color="#f1f6fb")
ax.tick_params(colors="#d5e1eb")
for spine in ax.spines.values():
    spine.set_color("#5d7590")
ax.grid(True, color="#7890a8", alpha=0.25)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The logarithmic vertical scale is necessary here: the chain reaches , far above its starting value.

Mathematical Ideas Connected with the Collatz Conjecture

The Collatz map is a discrete dynamical system: it studies the long-term behaviour of repeatedly applying a function on the positive integers. Questions about iteration include whether an orbit reaches a cycle, whether it escapes, and how long it takes to fall below its starting value.

The total stopping time is

When the orbit reaches , . A related stopping time is the first for which . Proving that every starting value eventually falls below itself would give a route to the conjecture by strong induction, but that statement is also unproved.

For odd , the accelerated Collatz map, often called the Syracuse map, divides all powers of two out of at once:

Here is the exponent of in . The map sends odd integers directly to odd integers. Parity sequences, or parity vectors, record the even and odd decisions along an orbit. Once a finite parity pattern is fixed, the iterate has an affine form involving powers of and ; the difficulty is that the pattern depends on the orbit itself.

The directed Collatz graph has an arrow from every positive integer. Reversing the arrows produces inverse Collatz trees, which make shared tails visible. Probabilistic heuristics model the powers of two dividing as if they were random; they help explain the observed downward tendency, but they are not proofs because actual parity events are deterministic and dependent.

There are important partial results. Terence Tao proved that, in a precise logarithmic-density sense, almost all orbits attain values below any function that tends to infinity. “Almost all” is not “all”, so this does not settle the original conjecture. Generalized Collatz problems can even be undecidable in some settings. That result concerns generalized systems; it does not show that the original Collatz conjecture is undecidable.

Conclusion

The Collatz conjecture remains open. Project Euler Problem 14 is nevertheless a finite problem, and the recurrence

turns its repeated tails into a practical memoized computation. The longest chain below one million starts at and has terms.

References

  1. Project Euler, Problem 14: Longest Collatz Sequence
  2. Jeffrey C. Lagarias, The Problem: An Overview
  3. Terence Tao, Almost All Orbits of the Collatz Map Attain Almost Bounded Values
  4. Terence Tao, expository discussion of the result
  5. Stuart A. Kurtz and Janos Simon, The Undecidability of the Generalized Collatz Problem

item.title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
item.dc:creator: Ashish K. Srivastava
item.description:
Introduction

Counting integer solutions is a central idea in algebra, number theory, and combinatorics. Here the word positive matters: each variable must be at least , not merely non-negative.

We will count the solutions in four complementary ways. The mathematics supplies the proof; Python provides an independent verification.

Difficulty: Intermediate
Prerequisites: Basic algebra, inequalities, and elementary counting
Estimated reading time: 8 minutes

Problem

Find the number of positive integer solutions of

where . The answer will be

Method 1: Fix One Variable and Count

Solving for gives

Since , we have . Because , this gives , so . Thus .

For each fixed , count the positive values of for which stays positive.

Value of Equation for and Allowed values of Number of solutions

Therefore,

Method 2: Convert Positive Integers to Non-negative Integers

Write

where . Substitution gives

and hence

For fixed , this becomes . An equation , with , has solutions because ranges from to .

Equation Number of solutions

Again,

Method 3: Direct Floor-Function Formula

For fixed positive integer , positivity of implies

so

The number of positive integer values of is therefore

Since ,

Evaluating the sum,

Method 4: Generating Functions

The positive values of , , and are encoded respectively by

Their product is

Choosing , , and from the three factors produces

Thus every positive solution contributes exactly one term, so the answer is

That coefficient is , in agreement with the direct counts.

Python Verification

The following program uses the equation to compute after choosing positive and . It prints the triples in the same order as the hand-counting method.

Hide code 
solutions = []

for z in range(1, 5):
    for y in range(1, 16):
        x = 15 - 2 * y - 3 * z
        if x > 0:
            solution = (x, y, z)
            solutions.append(solution)
            print(solution)

print("Total number of solutions:", len(solutions))
(10, 1, 1)
(8, 2, 1)
(6, 3, 1)
(4, 4, 1)
(2, 5, 1)
(7, 1, 2)
(5, 2, 2)
(3, 3, 2)
(1, 4, 2)
(4, 1, 3)
(2, 2, 3)
(1, 1, 4)
Total number of solutions: 12

The program confirms the count, but the earlier methods explain why the count is .

Complete List of Solutions

The complete solution list is

Every listed triple satisfies , and the list contains triples.

Comparison of Methods

Method Main idea Best use
Fix one variable Count directly after fixing Small equations and hand calculations
Shift variables Replace positivity by non-negativity Connecting with standard combinatorics
Floor formula Express each row count compactly General formulas
Generating functions Read a coefficient from a product of series Weighted counting problems

Conclusion

The four methods illuminate the same result from different angles: direct counting is concrete, shifting variables connects the problem to non-negative solutions, floor functions compress the count, and generating functions give a powerful general framework. Python verifies the final list.

Try It Yourself

  1. Find the number of positive integer solutions of .
  2. Find the number of non-negative integer solutions of .
  3. Find the number of positive integer solutions of .
TipHint for Problem 1

Fix first. For each allowed value of , count the positive values of that keep positive.

NoteSolution for Problem 1

The possible values are . The row counts are , so the total is

TipHint for Problem 2

Fix . For each , solve with .

NoteSolution for Problem 2

The row counts are , giving

TipHint for Problem 3

Use and count the positive values of for each possible positive .

NoteSolution for Problem 3

For , the possible values of number respectively. Hence the total is

item.title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
item.dc:creator: Ashish K. Srivastava
item.description:
The property

Let us explore one of the most useful symmetry ideas in definite integration. If is integrable on , King’s property says

In simple language: reflecting the graph horizontally across the midpoint does not change its signed area over the interval.

Adding the two equal forms gives the useful identity

Important: the property preserves signed area, so it remains valid even when parts of the graph lie below the -axis.

Let me prove it mathematically

Begin with the reflected integral

Now use the substitution

The limits reverse:

Therefore

In the last line, the dummy variable has simply been renamed as . This proves King’s property.

Now let us see the graphical proof

Focus on the transformation

It reflects every point across the midpoint . Imagine a very narrow strip at under . Its approximate signed area is

After reflection, its partner lies at . The reflected curve has height

Reflection also preserves the width . Therefore, the two strips have exactly the same signed area. Pairing every strip in this way shows that the two complete integrals must be equal.

Interactive area proof

Now try the idea yourself. Drag directly across the graph, or use the slider. The blue strip is at under ; the gold strip is at its reflected position under . Watch how the shaded regions accumulate equal signed areas.

Original strip: x = 1.50
Reflected strip: x = 4.50
Paired height: —
Accumulated areas: —

At the midpoint, the paired strips meet. Continue moving the slider to see them exchange sides while preserving their correspondence.

The applet uses and a deliberately asymmetric function. This makes it clear that the equality comes from reflection—not from the original graph already being symmetric.

See the applet code

The complete code behind the interactive graph is included below. Expand the block to study, copy, or adapt it.

NoteComplete JavaScript source 
(() => {
  const host = document.getElementById("king-svg");
  const slider = document.getElementById("king-position");
  if (!host || !slider) return;

  const NS = "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg";
  const a = 0, b = 6, W = 900, H = 470;
  const pad = { left: 58, right: 24, top: 28, bottom: 48 };
  const plotW = W - pad.left - pad.right;
  const plotH = H - pad.top - pad.bottom;
  const yMax = 3.15;
  const f = x => 1.35 + 0.42 * Math.sin(1.55 * x) + 0.16 * x;
  const reflected = x => f(a + b - x);
  const X = x => pad.left + (x - a) / (b - a) * plotW;
  const Y = y => pad.top + (yMax - y) / yMax * plotH;
  const base = Y(0);
  const els = {
    t: document.getElementById("king-t"),
    x: document.getElementById("king-x"),
    rx: document.getElementById("king-rx"),
    height: document.getElementById("king-height"),
    areas: document.getElementById("king-areas")
  };

  const svg = document.createElementNS(NS, "svg");
  svg.setAttribute("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`);
  svg.setAttribute("preserveAspectRatio", "xMidYMid meet");
  svg.setAttribute("aria-hidden", "true");
  host.appendChild(svg);

  const node = (name, attrs = {}, parent = svg) => {
    const el = document.createElementNS(NS, name);
    Object.entries(attrs).forEach(([key, value]) => el.setAttribute(key, value));
    parent.appendChild(el);
    return el;
  };
  const text = (value, x, y, attrs = {}) => {
    const el = node("text", { x, y, ...attrs });
    el.textContent = value;
    return el;
  };
  const points = (fn, lo = a, hi = b, count = 320) => {
    let d = "";
    for (let i = 0; i <= count; i++) {
      const x = lo + (hi - lo) * i / count;
      d += `${i ? "L" : "M"}${X(x).toFixed(2)},${Y(fn(x)).toFixed(2)} `;
    }
    return d;
  };
  const areaPath = (fn, lo, hi) => {
    const curve = points(fn, lo, hi, 150).replace(/^M/, "L");
    return `M${X(lo)},${base} ${curve}L${X(hi)},${base} L${X(lo)},${base} Z`;
  };
  const integral = (fn, lo, hi, n = 600) => {
    if (hi < lo) return -integral(fn, hi, lo, n);
    const h = (hi - lo) / n;
    let sum = 0.5 * (fn(lo) + fn(hi));
    for (let i = 1; i < n; i++) sum += fn(lo + i * h);
    return sum * h;
  };

  const grid = node("g", { stroke: "#aebdcb", "stroke-opacity": ".18" });
  for (let i = 0; i <= 6; i++) {
    node("line", { x1: X(i), y1: pad.top, x2: X(i), y2: base }, grid);
    text(String(i), X(i), base + 27, { fill: "#cbd8e4", "text-anchor": "middle" });
  }
  for (let y = 0; y <= 3; y++)
    node("line", { x1: pad.left, y1: Y(y), x2: W - pad.right, y2: Y(y) }, grid);

  node("line", { x1: pad.left, y1: base, x2: W-pad.right, y2: base, stroke: "#aebdcb" });
  node("line", { x1: X(3), y1: pad.top, x2: X(3), y2: base,
    stroke: "#ffffff", "stroke-opacity": ".35", "stroke-dasharray": "7 7" });
  text("midpoint", X(3), pad.top + 14, { fill: "#cbd8e4", "text-anchor": "middle" });
  text("f(x)", pad.left + 10, pad.top + 20, { fill: "#7be1eb", "font-weight": "700" });
  text("f(a+b−x)", pad.left + 10, pad.top + 42, { fill: "#ffd27a", "font-weight": "700" });

  const blueArea = node("path", { fill: "#57b9c5", "fill-opacity": ".22" });
  const goldArea = node("path", { fill: "#f3c56b", "fill-opacity": ".22" });
  node("path", { d: points(f), fill: "none", stroke: "#57b9c5", "stroke-width": "4" });
  node("path", { d: points(reflected), fill: "none", stroke: "#f3c56b", "stroke-width": "4" });
  const blueStrip = node("rect", { fill: "#7be1eb" });
  const goldStrip = node("rect", { fill: "#ffd27a" });
  const bluePoint = node("circle", { r: 7, fill: "#7be1eb" });
  const goldPoint = node("circle", { r: 7, fill: "#ffd27a" });

  function draw() {
    const t = Number(slider.value);
    const rt = a + b - t;
    const left = Math.min(t, rt), right = Math.max(t, rt);
    blueArea.setAttribute("d", areaPath(f, a, left));
    goldArea.setAttribute("d", areaPath(reflected, right, b));

    const setStrip = (rect, point, x, fn) => {
      const width = 10;
      rect.setAttribute("x", X(x) - width / 2);
      rect.setAttribute("y", Y(fn(x)));
      rect.setAttribute("width", width);
      rect.setAttribute("height", base - Y(fn(x)));
      point.setAttribute("cx", X(x));
      point.setAttribute("cy", Y(fn(x)));
    };
    setStrip(blueStrip, bluePoint, t, f);
    setStrip(goldStrip, goldPoint, rt, reflected);

    els.t.textContent = `t = ${t.toFixed(2)}`;
    els.x.textContent = `x = ${t.toFixed(2)}`;
    els.rx.textContent = `x = ${rt.toFixed(2)}`;
    els.height.textContent = `${f(t).toFixed(3)} = ${reflected(rt).toFixed(3)}`;
    els.areas.textContent =
      `${integral(f, a, left).toFixed(3)} ≈ ${integral(reflected, right, b).toFixed(3)}`;
  }

  function setFromPointer(event) {
    const rect = svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const viewX = (event.clientX - rect.left) / rect.width * W;
    const ratio = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, (viewX - pad.left) / plotW));
    slider.value = String(a + ratio * (b-a));
    draw();
  }

  let dragging = false;
  svg.addEventListener("pointerdown", event => {
    dragging = true;
    svg.setPointerCapture(event.pointerId);
    setFromPointer(event);
  });
  svg.addEventListener("pointermove", event => dragging && setFromPointer(event));
  svg.addEventListener("pointerup", () => dragging = false);
  svg.addEventListener("pointercancel", () => dragging = false);
  slider.addEventListener("input", draw);
  draw();
})();

Verifying the picture with Python

The same asymmetric function can be plotted and integrated numerically with Python. This gives an independent computational check of the graphical argument.

Show the Python code 
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.style.use("dark_background")

a, b = 0, 6
x = np.linspace(a, b, 1000)

def f(x):
    return 1.35 + 0.42 * np.sin(1.55 * x) + 0.16 * x

y_original = f(x)
y_reflected = f(a + b - x)

area_original = np.trapezoid(y_original, x)
area_reflected = np.trapezoid(y_reflected, x)

print(f"Area under f(x):       {area_original:.8f}")
print(f"Area after reflection: {area_reflected:.8f}")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5), facecolor="#0c1521")
ax.set_facecolor("#0c1521")
plt.plot(x, y_original, label=r"$f(x)$", linewidth=2.5)
plt.plot(x, y_reflected, label=r"$f(a+b-x)$", linewidth=2.5)
plt.fill_between(x, y_original, alpha=0.18)
plt.axvline((a + b) / 2, color="gray", linestyle="--", label="midpoint")
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.title("A function and its reflection")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Area under f(x):       11.51982490
Area after reflection: 11.51982490

The two printed values agree up to numerical rounding. Python confirms what the substitution already proved exactly.

Why the averaged form is powerful

Let

King’s property gives another expression for the same number:

Adding and dividing by gives

This is most useful when the sum inside the brackets simplifies.

Let me work through an example

Evaluate

Here . King’s property gives

Adding the two equations,

Hence

A more powerful JEE-style example

Evaluate

The factor makes direct integration look unpleasant. Reflecting across replaces it by , while . Thus

Adding this to the original form gives

Now rationalise the denominator:

Although the two terms are individually improper at , their difference has a finite continuous limit there. An antiderivative on each side is , and the combined definite integral equals . Therefore

The important move was not the final integration. King’s property first removed the troublesome factor .

Takeaway

King’s property tells us that a definite integral is unchanged when its interval is reflected. Algebraically, reversing the limits cancels the minus sign in . Geometrically, every signed-area strip has an equal reflected partner. The slider makes these two descriptions visible as the same idea.
